name: CNH Industrial N.V.

profile.description: CNH Industrial N.V. designs, manufactures, and distributes agricultural and construction equipment under the Case IH, New Holland, and Case Construction brands. Agriculture represents 82% of industrial net sales. The company also operates a financial services segment. Q1 2026 revenue was $3.8B with net income collapsing to $10M from $132M YoY as tariff headwinds (210-220 bps on ag margins, 600 bps on construction) compounded the worst agricultural equipment downturn in a decade.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (1/3): CNH has consistently launched precision agriculture technology — over 15 new tractor models, 10 harvesting platforms, and 30+ precision tech releases planned through 2027 — but these are incremental product refreshes within the existing agricultural equipment model rather than transformative business pivots [1]. The 2022 Iveco Group spin-off simplified the business but did not redefine it. AI Deployment (2/2): CNH's 'AI Tech Assistant' is deployed to hundreds of agricultural and construction dealer groups for real-time diagnostic support, and every member of the global leadership team is running at least one generative or agentic AI pilot program [1]. The company pioneered LNG-powered tractors and methane-powered production equipment. Market Share (1/2): CNH is a meaningful global competitor alongside John Deere and AGCO in agricultural equipment, with strong brand equity through Case IH and New Holland, but is not the category leader. Product Pipeline (1/2): Specific product launches through 2027 are named with timeline visibility, including precision ag tools and autonomous equipment features. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending, while increasing, remains below Deere's intensity level and is not top-quartile for the heavy equipment sector.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): CNH's 'Breaking New Ground' purpose connects equipment manufacturing to sustainable food production and global food security — a specific and thoughtful mission [2]. CEO Gerrit Marx has explicitly framed precision agriculture as a generational mission, stating that 'tomorrow's agronomist will be agentic AI helping farmers make bigger decisions better' [1]. The mission shapes R&D investment and product strategy. However, Marx's 1.75-year tenure means the purpose has not yet been tested through extended adversity. Community Impact (0/2): CNH participates in global sustainability initiatives including UN Sustainable Development Goals and has pioneered alternative fuel tractors, but no distinctive charitable programs with specific dollar amounts were identified [3]. Employee Retention (0/2): Glassdoor ratings of 3.7/5 overall with 3.3/5 for culture and values suggest a mediocre work environment. Reviews cite poor management, bureaucratic silos, and the company being 'slow to innovate' and 'content to follow market leaders' [2]. Employee development is described as lacking. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Marx's $11.7M total compensation is 89% equity-based, demonstrating alignment, but no meaningful net insider buying beyond scheduled compensation was identified.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored as a Growth stock (yield 0.94% < 2%, no 5+ year consistent growth history). D/E of 334% far exceeds the 300% floor, scoring 0 — CNH's financial services arm drives elevated leverage typical of captive finance companies but penalized under the standard framework [4]. ROE of 4.8% is weak, scoring 0.32 on 2x weight. Revenue growth is flat YoY (-0.1%) with a deeply negative 3-year CAGR of -8.4% as revenues fell from $23.6B (2022) to $18.1B (2025), both scoring near floor. Forward P/E of 14.6 is strong, scoring at target. Profit margin of 2.1% is near floor. The bright spot is Forward PEG of 0.61, well below the 1.0 target (2x weight), reflecting analyst expectations of significant earnings recovery from the current trough [4]. Q1 2026 showed continued pressure with net income of just $10M and an industrial adjusted EBIT loss of $45M driven by tariff impacts [1].

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9 — PROTECTED): CNH manufactures physical heavy equipment — tractors, combines, excavators — that cannot be replaced by software. The business involves capital-intensive physical products, complex dealer networks spanning thousands of locations globally, aftermarket parts and service, and a captive financial services arm, all of which are structurally immune to AI substitution [1]. Agricultural and construction equipment demand is driven by physical-world food production and infrastructure needs that AI amplifies rather than displaces. Precision agriculture technology powered by AI actually increases the value proposition of CNH's equipment platform. Analysts have flagged zero AI displacement risk for the heavy equipment sector. AI Opportunity (1/1): CNH's AI Tech Assistant deployed to hundreds of dealer groups generates measurable operational value, and every leadership team member runs generative or agentic AI pilots [1]. The precision agriculture platform represents genuine AI-powered revenue enhancement, though specific AI revenue figures have not been disclosed.

infinite_game.summary: CNH has a genuine long-term purpose around sustainable agriculture and food security that shapes R&D investment and product strategy even during the worst agricultural equipment downturn in a decade. The Iveco spin-off demonstrated strategic simplification courage. However, the company's downturn response has been cautious cost management rather than transformative investment, and CEO Marx's short tenure means the organization's infinite game commitment has not been tested through a full cycle.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): CNH's 'Breaking New Ground' purpose connects equipment manufacturing to sustainable food production and feeding a growing world. CEO Marx has explicitly framed precision agriculture as a generational mission and continued R&D investment through the downturn [1]. The mission shapes visible strategic decisions — alternative fuel tractors, AI-powered farming tools, and the Iveco spin-off to focus purely on agriculture and construction. However, the cause has not been validated across decades or leadership transitions — Marx has been CEO for only 1.75 years.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): CNH's 34,197 employees operate across a global manufacturing and dealer network with adequate organizational stability. The 2025 leadership reorganization under Marx was orderly, and Glassdoor's 74% recommendation rate suggests most employees view the company favorably [2]. However, culture ratings of 3.3/5 and reviews citing poor management, bureaucratic silos, and lack of innovation commitment suggest average trust at best.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CNH explicitly benchmarks against John Deere and AGCO, framing its product roadmap in terms of capability gaps to close rather than competitors to destroy. Management language focuses on delivering better products and improving underlying margins despite the downturn — a self-improvement orientation that acknowledges rivals' strengths [1]. The competitive framing is about catching Deere's precision agriculture lead through better technology rather than winning a market share war.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The 2022 Iveco Group spin-off was a significant strategic simplification, abandoning the commercial vehicle business to create a pure-play agriculture and construction company [1]. The shift from pure iron manufacturer to precision agriculture platform provider represents meaningful strategic evolution. However, these changes are incremental rather than transformative — CNH has not fundamentally reinvented its business model in the way Sinek's framework rewards.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Despite the worst agricultural equipment downturn in a decade and Q1 2026 near-breakeven results, CNH maintained 15+ new tractor launches and 30+ precision tech releases through 2027, continuing to invest in capability [1]. CEO Marx's reaffirmation of full-year 2026 guidance despite Q1 weakness signals conviction. The Iveco spin-off required courage to shrink the company. However, the tariff response has been defensive (cost management, supply chain adjustment) rather than offensive investment.

investment_thesis: CNH Industrial trades at 14.6x forward P/E with a PEG of 0.61, pricing in significant earnings recovery from the current agricultural equipment trough [4]. The company reaffirmed full-year 2026 guidance of -4% to flat industrial net sales and 2.5-3.5% adjusted EBIT margin, with Q1 expected as the low point [1]. North America remains the profit engine, and precision agriculture technology (30+ releases through 2027, AI Tech Assistant deployed to dealer network) positions CNH to capture more value per unit as the cycle turns. The Inspire Impact Score of 64 (ALIGNED) reflects industry-leading ESG practices across 17 positive attributions. The Iveco spin-off created a focused pure-play with a genuine long-term purpose around food security.
thesis_continued: The near-term outlook is severely challenged. Revenue has fallen from $24.7B (2023) to $18.1B (2025), a -8.4% 3-year CAGR, with further declines guided for 2026 [4]. Q1 2026 net income collapsed to $10M from $132M YoY, and the industrial segment posted a $45M adjusted EBIT loss due to tariff impacts of 210-220 bps on agriculture and 600 bps on construction margins [1]. D/E of 334% reflects captive finance leverage but constrains financial flexibility. Global agricultural equipment demand is expected to decline another 5% in 2026. Profit margins of 2.1% and ROE of 4.8% indicate the company is earning inadequate returns on its massive capital base. CEO Marx's 1.75-year tenure means the current strategy is unproven through a full cycle, and Glassdoor reviews describe the company as 'slow to innovate' and 'content to follow market leaders' [2].
key_catalysts: Agricultural equipment demand recovery beginning in 2027 as dealer inventory destocking completes and farmer income stabilizes, creating significant operating leverage on CNH's fixed cost structure
Precision agriculture technology monetization — AI Tech Assistant and integrated crop management platform driving software/service revenue at margins above hardware business
Tariff resolution or supply chain restructuring eliminating the 210-600 bps margin headwind that depressed Q1 2026 results
key_risks: Agricultural equipment downturn extending beyond 2026 with global demand declining a further 5%, creating additional earnings pressure on already-thin margins and potentially forcing restructuring charges

Tariff escalation on CNH's cross-border supply chain (manufacturing in US, UK, Brazil, India) creating persistent margin headwinds that management estimated at 210-220 bps on agriculture and 600 bps on construction
John Deere's autonomous and electric equipment roadmap and superior precision agriculture ecosystem potentially widening the competitive gap, relegating CNH to a value-tier follower position
